text: In casual speech, {{c1::want to}} often becomes {{c2::wanna}}, and {{c3::going to}} becomes {{c4::gonna}}.
explanation: These reductions are very common in everyday conversation and are part of natural connected speech. They sound smoother and less formal.
usage:
Pattern: wanna / gonna
  • I wanna grab coffee.
  • We’re gonna be late.
spanish: En el habla casual, 'want to' suele convertirse en 'wanna' y 'going to' en 'gonna'.

text: The phrase {{c1::I don’t know}} is often reduced to {{c2::I dunno}} in fast speech.
explanation: Connected speech frequently reduces function words and weakens vowels. This helps speech sound more natural and less mechanical.
usage:
Pattern: I dunno / I don’t know
  • I dunno what to do.
  • I don’t know yet.
spanish: La frase 'I don’t know' suele reducirse a 'I dunno' en el habla rápida.

text: In casual speech, {{c1::sort of}} and {{c2::kind of}} are often pronounced as weaker, faster chunks.
explanation: These phrases are common when speakers want to soften a statement or sound less absolute.
usage:
Pattern: sort of / kind of
  • I’m sort of tired.
  • It’s kind of expensive.
spanish: En el habla casual, 'sort of' y 'kind of' suelen sonar como grupos más débiles y rápidos.

scenario: Phonetics: Gonna / Wanna in Real Speech 🎧
text: When speakers say {{c1::wanna}} or {{c2::gonna}}, the consonants and vowels blend together to create a smoother rhythm.
explanation: This is one of the clearest examples of connected speech. The result is a shorter, more fluid sentence.
usage:
Pattern: gonna / wanna
  • I wanna leave.
  • She’s gonna call later.
spanish: Cuando los hablantes dicen 'wanna' o 'gonna', las consonantes y vocales se mezclan para crear un ritmo más fluido.

text: Function words like {{c1::to}}, {{c2::of}}, and {{c3::and}} often reduce to a weak schwa sound in fast speech.
explanation: This is a key feature of natural English rhythm and helps learners sound more native-like.
usage:
Pattern: schwa reduction
  • What do you want?
  • I’m gonna go to the store.
spanish: Palabras funcionales como 'to', 'of' y 'and' a menudo se reducen a un sonido débil en el habla rápida.

text: When one word ends in a consonant and the next begins with a vowel, the sound often {{c1::links}} smoothly.
explanation: Linking helps create the fluid rhythm of spoken English. It is especially useful in casual everyday phrases.
spanish: Cuando una palabra termina en consonante y la siguiente empieza con vocal, el sonido suele enlazarse suavemente.

text: Native speakers usually give the most important word in the sentence the strongest {{c1::stress}}.
explanation: Sentence stress is crucial for clarity and intelligibility. It also helps listeners understand the meaning instantly.
usage:
Pattern: sentence stress / content words
  • I WANT a coffee.
  • We’re GOING to the airport.
spanish: Los hablantes nativos normalmente dan mayor énfasis a la palabra más importante de la oración.

text: Natural English often comes in {{c1::chunks}} rather than isolated words, especially in everyday speech.
explanation: Chunking helps learners process phrases like 'gonna grab a coffee' as one smooth unit instead of several separate words.
usage:
Pattern: chunking / phrase stress
  • gonna grab a coffee
  • let me think
spanish: El inglés natural suele venir en bloques de expresión en lugar de palabras aisladas, especialmente en el habla cotidiana.
